questions.text: Which network topology connects all devices to a single central switch?
questions.explanation: In a star topology, all devices connect to a central switch. Data passes through the switch to reach its destination.

questions.text: What is a major disadvantage of the bus topology?

options.text: It requires the most cabling of any topology
It is the most expensive topology
If the backbone cable fails, the entire network fails

questions.explanation: In a bus topology, all devices share a single backbone cable. If this cable fails or is damaged, all devices lose network connectivity.

questions.text: What is the difference between a switch and a hub?

options.text: A hub sends data only to the intended device; a switch broadcasts to all
A switch sends data only to the intended device; a hub broadcasts to all
A switch connects different networks; a hub connects devices in a LAN
There is no difference; they are the same device

questions.explanation: A switch is intelligent — it sends data only to the destination device, reducing unnecessary traffic. A hub broadcasts data to all connected devices.

questions.text: Which protocol is responsible for translating domain names to IP addresses?
questions.explanation: DNS (Domain Name System) translates human-readable domain names (e.g. google.com) into IP addresses that computers can use to route packets.

questions.text: Which protocol guarantees reliable, ordered delivery of data?
questions.explanation: TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) provides reliable, ordered delivery. It checks for errors and resends lost packets. UDP sacrifices reliability for speed.

questions.text: Why is HTTPS more secure than HTTP?

options.text: HTTPS uses UDP instead of TCP
HTTPS encrypts data using TLS/SSL to prevent interception
HTTPS sends data in smaller packets

questions.explanation: HTTPS adds TLS/SSL encryption to HTTP. This means data in transit cannot be read by a third party intercepting the connection (man-in-the-middle attack).

questions.text: What is a key advantage of packet switching over sending data as one large block?

options.text: Packets are always larger than blocks
Packets must all take the same route
Packets can reroute around network failures and congestion

questions.explanation: Packet switching allows each packet to travel independently. If one route is congested or a router fails, packets can take alternative paths — increasing resilience.

questions.text: A student wants their emails synchronised across a laptop, phone, and tablet. Which email protocol should they use?
questions.explanation: IMAP keeps emails on the server and synchronises them across all devices. POP3 downloads and deletes from the server, so emails are only on one device.

questions.text: Which layer of the TCP/IP model handles logical addressing and routing of packets?
questions.explanation: The Internet layer uses IP for logical addressing and routing packets between networks. The Transport layer handles reliable delivery (TCP/UDP).

questions.text: Why was IPv6 developed to replace IPv4?

options.text: IPv4 was too slow for modern networks
IPv4's ~4.3 billion addresses are nearly exhausted

questions.explanation: IPv4 uses 32-bit addresses, giving ~4.3 billion possible addresses — insufficient for the modern internet. IPv6 uses 128-bit addresses, giving ~340 undecillion addresses.

options.text: Connects devices within the same LAN
Connects different networks and directs packets between them
Provides Wi-Fi access to wireless devices

questions.explanation: A router connects different networks (e.g. your home LAN to the internet) and uses routing tables to direct packets along the best path to their destination.

questions.text: Which topology is most resilient to hardware failures?
questions.explanation: Mesh topology has multiple connections between devices. If one link or device fails, data can reroute through alternative paths, making it the most resilient topology.
